justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-4 and the opening business_context both call out that this fundamentally changes Sales context autonomy by introducing a synchronous dependency on Warehouse state. (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-4, BR-7, and the ubiquitous language clearly establish Warehouse as the owner of StockLevel and define 'Available Stock' as a derived concept. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — AC-3 directly tests concurrent orders for the last items, BR-2 explains why naive reads cause oversell, and BR-5 explicitly states the read model is eventually consistent and must not be treated as a guarantee. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is introduced as a new concept in the ubiquitous language with the note 'Does not exist yet — must be introduced,' and BR-2/BR-7 formalize the reservation mechanism. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — four escalations cover the integration pattern, consistency model, partial fulfillment policy, and failure modes, all of which are architectural decisions requiring MJ input. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-1, BR-2, BR-4, and BR-7 are all marked as invariants governing cross-context behavior.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis. The ubiquitous language section introduces 'Stock Reservation' and 'Available Stock' as new concepts that don't yet exist, correctly identifying the gap in the current domain model.

TOCTOU is addressed at multiple levels: BR-5 flags the stale-read problem, BR-7 establishes that the authoritative decision must be made by Warehouse (the data owner), and AC-3 provides a concrete concurrent-order test scenario.
Context coupling analysis is first-class. BR-4 is an explicit invariant that Sales must never directly read/write Warehouse data, with a testable assertion that no code path crosses the boundary.

Reservation vs. check distinction is clearly surfaced. The confidence section explicitly notes 'without reservations the feature is fundamentally broken under concurrency' and lists it as an assumption since the spec doesn't mention reservations.
Strong edge cases including inventory shrinkage below existing reservations, product mismatch between contexts, and backpressure on the read model — these go well beyond the minimum requirements.
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the number of unresolved ambiguities, and the assumptions are explicitly listed rather than silently baked into acceptance criteria.
Escalations are structured with concrete options and recommendations rather than vague 'needs discussion' flags, making them actionable for MJ.

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-2 and BR-6 frame it as an architectural invariant, rejection reasons call out synchronous coupling; (2) Data ownership question raised — escalation explicitly asks who owns the Stock Reservation aggregate, and the ubiquitous language notes Product means different things in each context; (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — BR-4 directly states 'two concurrent orders can both see stock=1, both pass the check, and both get accepted', AC-4 tests it; (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — Stock Reservation defined as 'currently MISSING', BR-4 frames it as invariant, AC-1/AC-5 cover reservation lifecycle; (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling — four structured escalations covering multi-line fulfillment, real-time vs eventual consistency, reservation ownership, and TTL behavior; (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-4 explicitly marks reservation atomicity as invariant.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on context coupling — BR-2 frames bounded context autonomy as an invariant and AC-3 tests graceful degradation when Context A is unavailable, going beyond what the rubric required
Introduced Available-to-Promise (ATP) as a missing domain concept, which is the correct abstraction for the 'available stock' ownership question
TOCTOU identified precisely with a concrete testable assertion in BR-4 and a corresponding acceptance criterion AC-4
Stock Reservation explicitly called out as MISSING from current domain model, with TTL lifecycle, expiry edge cases, and the reservation-during-payment ambiguity all surfaced
Rejection reasons are well-formed — they connect the naive implementation back to specific business rules that would be violated
Edge cases are strong — supplier receipt race, multi-line partial fulfillment, missing StockLevel record, and stale indicator at checkout are all real-world scenarios
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the significant ambiguities identified — demonstrates intellectual honesty rather than false certainty
Proposed a new bounded context (Inventory Allocation) for reservation ownership rather than forcing it into either existing context — sophisticated DDD thinking

This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling problem that fundamentally changes the autonomy of both contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request implicitly proposes merging concerns t

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — Bird calls out 'cross-context coupling challenge' and notes this 'changes the core domain model of the Sales context.' (2) Data ownership questioned — 'Available Stock' flagged as undefined with dedicated escalation on who owns it and three options for its definition. (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — BR-4 directly states the concurrent order problem with a concrete example (two orders of 3 against stock of 5), and edge case #1 elaborates further. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation / Allocation' listed as NOT YET DEFINED in ubiquitous language, escalation recommends introducing a Reservation concept (Option B). (5) Escalation to MJ implicit via structured escalations with architectural coupling patterns (anti-corruption layer, context communication model). (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-4 is an explicit cross-context invariant with invariant:true.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — identifies 'Available Stock' as undefined and provides three concrete definitions (on-hand, minus-allocated, ATP) with business tradeoffs for each.
TOCTOU problem fully explained with concrete scenario (two simultaneous orders for last 5 units) and three resolution mechanisms (pessimistic locking, optimistic concurrency, reservation).
Identifies the contradiction between current behavior ('accept all orders') and the new feature ('conditionally accept'), flagging this as a fundamental domain model change.

Goes beyond rubric requirements: identifies partial vs. all-or-nothing order blocking, Warehouse unavailability failure modes, and post-acceptance stock adjustment scenarios — all genuinely important edge cases.
Correctly withholds acceptance criteria, noting they cannot be defined until ambiguities are resolved — avoids the failure mode of silently resolving undefined questions.
Appropriate 40% confidence level reflects the high ambiguity in the spec.

Escalations are not explicitly addressed 'to MJ' by name — they are structured escalations but lack the direct agent routing. This is a minor gap since the escalation content is architecturally substantive.

This is a classic cross-context coupling challenge in logistics and retail domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
